mnemonics.title: APIE - The OOP Four Pillars
mnemonics.content: A-PIE: Abstraction, Polymorphism, Inheritance, Encapsulation - These are the four pillars that hold up the entire building of Object Oriented Programming in Python.

mnemonics.title: Don't Repeat Yourself
mnemonics.content: DRY: Don't Repeat Yourself - If you find yourself writing the same code twice, create a function or method instead. Remember: Copy-paste is a sin in programming!

mnemonics.title: Keep It Simple Stupid
mnemonics.content: KISS: Keep It Simple, Stupid - Simple solutions are easier to debug, maintain, and understand. Complex code breeds bugs.

mnemonics.title: You Aren't Gonna Need It
mnemonics.content: YAGNI: You Aren't Gonna Need It - Don't build features or structures 'just in case.' Solve the problem at hand first.

mnemonics.title: The Problem Solver's Recipe
mnemonics.content: Understand → Plan → Code → Test → Debug: UPCTD - First understand the problem completely, then plan your solution, write the code, test thoroughly, and debug if needed.

mnemonics.title: SOLID Design Principles
mnemonics.content: SOLID: Single responsibility, Open-closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion - Follow these five principles to write maintainable, flexible OOP code.

mnemonics.title: The Debugging Dance
mnemonics.content: Read, Reproduce, Isolate, Find, Fix: RR IFF - Read the error message carefully, reproduce the bug consistently, isolate the problematic code, find the root cause, then fix it properly.

acronyms.usage: Programming paradigm based on objects containing data (attributes) and code (methods)
Principle to avoid code duplication; use functions, classes, or inheritance instead
Keep solutions simple and straightforward; avoid unnecessary complexity
Don't implement features until they are actually required

acronyms.expansion: Single responsibility, Open-closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion
acronyms.usage: Five design principles for writing maintainable and scalable OOP code
The four fundamental pillars/concepts of Object Oriented Programming
Software application providing comprehensive facilities for software development (e.g., PyCharm, VS Code)
Executes bytecode compiled from Python source code; where your OOP code actually runs

visualTips.visualization: Picture a class as an architectural blueprint for a house. The blueprint itself isn't a house, but it defines how houses should be built. All houses (objects) created from this blueprint will have the same structure.

Imagine an actual physical house built from the blueprint - that's an object. Multiple houses (objects) can be built from one blueprint (class), each being a unique instance with potentially different attribute values (color, size).

Think of methods as action words or verbs in a sentence. Just as 'run', 'eat', 'jump' describe actions, methods like 'calculate_total()', 'print_receipt()', or 'move_player()' describe what objects can do.

Visualize attributes as adjectives or nouns describing characteristics. A 'Car' class might have attributes: color (red), speed (0), model ('Sedan') - these are the properties that describe the object.
Picture a family tree where children inherit traits from parents. In OOP, a 'Dog' class might inherit from an 'Animal' class, getting all general animal behaviors plus its own specific dog behaviors.

Imagine a medicine capsule - it protects the medicine inside. Similarly, encapsulation wraps data (attributes) and methods together, protecting the internal state from outside interference using access modifiers (public, private, protected).

Think of a shape-shifting actor who can play many roles. In OOP, polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common superclass. Like a 'draw()' method works differently for Circle, Square, and Triangle.
Picture a TV remote - you press buttons without knowing the complex electronics inside. Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only the necessary features of an object.

analogies.analogy: Eating an elephant one bite at a time - A huge problem seems impossible until you break it into smaller, manageable pieces. In OOP, you decompose a large system into classes and methods.
A recipe card - A recipe defines what ingredients (attributes) and instructions (methods) are needed. Following the recipe creates actual dishes (objects). One recipe can create many dishes.

Genetic inheritance in families - Children inherit features from parents. A 'Student' class might inherit from a 'Person' class, getting name and age attributes automatically while adding student-specific features like roll_number.
A bank vault - Money and valuables are protected inside a vault with controlled access. Similarly, private data is protected inside a class with controlled access through public methods.
A traffic signal - The same signal concept (red light) means 'stop' for cars, 'wait' for pedestrians, and 'halt' for trains. One interface, different behaviors based on context.
Parent teaching child a skill - The parent shows a general way to do something, but the child adapts it to their own style. Child class overrides parent method with its own implementation.
Birth certificate - When a person (object) is born, their name, date of birth, and other details are recorded. Similarly, __init__() initializes new objects with their initial attributes.
Detective solving a mystery - You gather clues (error messages), reproduce the crime (bug), narrow down suspects (problematic code), find the culprit (root cause), and solve the case (fix the bug).
